sections.title: What phrasal verbs are (core logic)
sections.content:
A **phrasal verb** is a verb + small word (a **particle** — usually a preposition or adverb) that together create a **NEW meaning**, often very different from the literal parts. *Look up* (look + up) doesn't mean look upwards — it means **search for information**.

**Why it matters (roots-first)**: Phrasal verbs sit in the Prepositions & Particles root (#6 in the Tree Model) — their primary home since the 2026 reorganisation, with a secondary link to Verb Complementation. The particle is what changes the meaning: same verb, different particle, different verb entirely (*give up* / *give in* / *give back* / *give out*). For Czech speakers, phrasal verbs are the **spoken/informal heart** of English — avoiding them makes you sound stiff and textbook-formal; using them well is what natural English actually sounds like.

**Key principle**: learn the whole phrase + meaning + an example sentence. Don't translate word-for-word.

sections.title: Literal vs idiomatic meaning
sections.content:
Some phrasal verbs are **literal** — the meaning is roughly the verb + the particle's normal sense. Most are **idiomatic** — the meaning is unpredictable and must be learned as a chunk.

**Why it matters**: this is where word-for-word translation fails hardest. *Bring up* is not about bringing anything upwards — it means raise (children) or mention (a topic). Same verb + different particles can produce five totally different verbs.

**Contrast examples (literal vs idiomatic)**:
- *She walked **across** the road.* (literal — actual movement across)
- *She **brought up** the issue at the meeting.* (idiomatic — mentioned)
- *The plane **took off** at 6.* (idiomatic — left the ground)
- *He **took off** his coat.* (semi-literal — removed)

**One verb, many meanings — TAKE + particle**:
- *take off* = leave (plane) / remove (clothes)
- *take after* = resemble (a parent)
- *take on* = accept (a job/challenge)
- *take in* = understand / deceive / make smaller
- *take over* = assume control

Same verb. Five different verbs. The particle does all the work.

sections.title: Transitive vs intransitive
sections.content:
Like all verbs, phrasal verbs are either **intransitive** (no object) or **transitive** (need an object). Many work both ways — with different meanings.

**Why it matters**: knowing whether a phrasal verb takes an object is the first step before worrying about word order. Get this wrong and the sentence breaks.

**Intransitive (no object)**:
- *The plane **took off**.* (left the ground)
- *Please **sit down**.*
- *They **broke up** last year.* (ended their relationship)

**Transitive (needs an object)**:
- *She **made up** a story.* (invented)
- *They **called off** the match.* (cancelled)
- *I **looked up** the word.* (searched for)

**Both — with different meanings**:
- *She **turned up** late.* (arrived — intransitive)
- *She **turned up** the volume.* (increased — transitive)
- *He **gave up**.* (stopped trying — intransitive)
- *He **gave up** smoking.* (quit — transitive)

items.good: The plane took off at 6. (intransitive — no object)
She made up a story. (transitive — needs object)
She turned up late. (intransitive) / She turned up the volume. (transitive — different meaning)

sections.title: Separable vs inseparable — the position rule
sections.content:
For **transitive** phrasal verbs, where does the object go? This is the highest-value mechanical rule.

**Separable** (most common): the object can go **before OR after** the particle.
- *Turn **on** the light.* / *Turn the light **on**.* — both fine.

**BUT** if the object is a **pronoun** (it, him, her, them...), it **MUST** go between the verb and the particle.
- *Turn **it** on.* ✓
- *Turn on **it**.* ✗

**Inseparable** (a smaller set): the verb + particle stay glued together. The object always comes after.
- *Look **after** the children.* ✓
- *Look the children **after**.* ✗
- *Look **after** them.* ✓ (pronoun also after the particle)

**Why it matters**: pronoun word order is the #1 error pattern. Native speakers immediately hear *turn on it* as wrong. There's no reliable rule for which verbs are separable vs inseparable — learn each one as you meet it.

diagrams.title: Separable vs inseparable — object position
rows: **Separable** (e.g. *turn on*)
Turn on the light / Turn the light on
Turn **it** on (NOT *turn on it*)
**Inseparable** (e.g. *look after*)
Look after the children
Look after **them** (NOT *look them after*)
**Intransitive** (e.g. *take off*)
no object — The plane took off

sections.title: Three-part phrasal verbs
sections.content:
Some phrasal verbs are **three words**: verb + adverb + preposition. They're **always inseparable** — the three parts stay together, and the object comes after.

**Common examples**:
- *look forward to* (anticipate with pleasure)
- *put up with* (tolerate)
- *get away with* (escape consequences)
- *come up with* (think of, invent)
- *run out of* (use up)
- *get on with* (have a good relationship with)
- *catch up with* (reach the same level as)

**Why it matters**: these are extremely common in spoken English and exam open-cloze. They're also a frequent trap: students often drop the third word (*She came up a solution* instead of *came up with a solution*).

**Gerund trap after *to***: when *to* is the preposition (not the infinitive marker), the next verb takes **-ing**:
- *I look forward **to seeing** you.* ✓ (NOT *to see*)
- *He's used **to working** late.* ✓
- *She objects **to paying** extra.* ✓

This catches almost every learner. *To* here is a preposition — it needs a noun or gerund, not an infinitive.

diagrams.text: Gerund trap: when 'to' is the preposition (not the infinitive marker), the next verb takes -ing. 'I look forward to SEEING you.' 'She objects to PAYING extra.' 'He's used to WORKING late.'

sections.title: High-frequency families
sections.content:
A handful of very common verbs produce dozens of phrasal verbs. Learning these families gives you a huge return for the effort.

**Why it matters**: these are the phrasal verbs you'll hear in every conversation, film, and podcast. Building familiarity with the families is faster than learning isolated items — the verb stays the same, only the particle (and meaning) changes.

diagrams.title: High-frequency phrasal verb families
rows: go on / go off / go through / go over
continue / explode or stop working / experience or examine / review
**GET**
get up / get on / get over / get away
rise / board (a bus) or progress / recover / escape
**TAKE**
take off / take after / take in / take over
leave (plane) / resemble / understand or deceive / assume control
**MAKE**
make up / make out / make for
invent or reconcile / understand with difficulty / head towards
**TURN**
turn on / turn off / turn up / turn down / turn into
switch on / switch off / arrive or increase / refuse or lower / become
**PUT**
put on / put off / put up with / put away
wear / postpone / tolerate / store

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors & strategies
sections.content:
**High-frequency mistakes** (often L1 interference or avoidance):

- **Literal translation** — calquing the Czech preposition or particle word-for-word instead of learning the English chunk.
- **Separating an inseparable verb** — *Look the children after* → **Look after the children.**
- **Noun-in-middle when pronoun is required** — *Turn on it* → **Turn it on.**
- **Dropping the third word** — *She came up a solution* → **She came up with a solution.**
- **Over-using the formal one-word alternative** — *postpone* over *put off*, *establish* over *set up*, *discover* over *find out*. Correct, but sounds stiff and bookish in everyday speech.
- **Wrong particle, similar verb** — *put on* (wear) vs *put off* (postpone); *look up* (search) vs *look after* (care for) vs *look for* (search for).

**Strategy — learn in context, not lists**:

1. Learn each phrasal verb as a **whole chunk in a sentence**, not as an isolated word with a definition.
2. Note the **pattern** (separable / inseparable / three-part) the first time you meet it.
3. Practise in **speaking and watching** (TV series, films, podcasts) — phrasal verbs are spoken-register, so input must match.
4. **Don't avoid them** — using a one-word formal verb every time is the textbook-trap. Phrasal verbs are how natural English sounds.

**Why it matters**: phrasal verbs are tested heavily in B2 / C1 exams (open cloze, key-word transformations) and they're the difference between sounding fluent and sounding like a phrasebook.

items.why: pronoun MUST go between verb and particle

items.good: She came up with a solution.
items.why: three-part verbs — never drop the third word

questions.question: 'We need to _____ the meeting.' (postpone) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Put off = postpone. Put on = wear; put up = raise; put down = place down.

questions.question: 'Who will _____ the children?' (take care of) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Look after = take care of. Look up = search; look for = search for.

questions.question: 'Don't _____ !' (stop trying) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Give up = stop trying. Give in = yield; give out = distribute; give back = return.

questions.question: 'We've _____ milk.' (used up) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Run out of = use up, have none left. Run into = meet by chance; run over = hit.

questions.question: '_____ the light.' (switch on) With a pronoun: _____
questions.explanation: Separable phrasal verb: pronoun goes between verb and particle (Turn it on).

questions.question: 'She _____ a solution.' (thought of an idea) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Come up with = think of (an idea). Come across = find by chance; come down with = become ill.

questions.question: Please _____ with your work. (continue)
questions.explanation: Go on = continue.

questions.question: They _____ the experiment. (did, completed)
questions.explanation: Carry out = do or complete a task.

questions.question: We _____ a meeting for Monday. (arranged)
questions.explanation: Set up = establish, arrange.

questions.question: I'll _____ you at seven. (collect)
questions.explanation: Pick up = collect. Separable: pick you up.

questions.question: Don't _____ ! (stop trying)
questions.explanation: Give up = stop trying.

questions.question: _____ the word in a dictionary. (search for)
questions.explanation: Look up = search for information.

questions.question: Who will _____ the children? (take care of)
questions.explanation: Look after = take care of.

questions.question: We _____ the meeting. Replace 'postponed' with a two-word phrasal verb that means the same.
questions.explanation: Put off = postpone.

questions.question: _____ your homework. (submit)
questions.explanation: Hand in = submit.

questions.question: We've _____ of milk. (used up)
questions.explanation: Run out of = use up, have none left.

questions.question: They _____ the match. (cancelled)
questions.explanation: Call off = cancel.

questions.question: I _____ at seven. (rise from bed)
questions.explanation: Get up = rise from bed or seat.

questions.question: _____ your coat. (wear)
questions.explanation: Put on = wear; switch on.

questions.question: _____ the light. (switch off)
questions.explanation: Turn off = switch off.

questions.question: Things will _____. (succeed)
questions.explanation: Work out = succeed; exercise; calculate.

questions.question: 'Look up' means:
questions.explanation: Look up = search for information (e.g. in a dictionary).

questions.question: 'Put off' means:
questions.explanation: Put off = postpone.

questions.question: 'Take off' can mean:
questions.explanation: Take off = remove clothes; (plane) leave the ground.

questions.question: 'Run out of' means:
questions.explanation: Run out of = use up, have no more.

questions.question: 'Bring up' can mean:
questions.explanation: Bring up = raise children; mention a topic.

questions.question: 'Go through' means:
questions.explanation: Go through = experience; examine.

questions.question: 'Find out' means:
questions.explanation: Find out = discover.

questions.question: 'Take over' means:
questions.explanation: Take over = assume control.

questions.question: 'Set up' means:
questions.explanation: Set up = establish, arrange.

questions.question: 'Carry on' means:
questions.explanation: Carry on = continue.

questions.question: He _____ a solution. (thought of)
questions.explanation: Come up with = think of an idea.

questions.question: I _____ an old friend. (met by chance)
questions.explanation: Bump into = meet by chance.

questions.question: She _____ flu. (became ill with)
questions.explanation: Come down with = become ill with.

questions.question: I _____ an interesting article. (found by chance)
questions.explanation: Come across = find by chance; seem.

questions.question: They _____ last year. (ended their relationship)
questions.explanation: Break up = end a relationship; disperse.

questions.question: We _____ at home. (finally were)
questions.explanation: End up = finally do or be.

questions.question: I can't _____ this problem. (solve)
questions.explanation: Figure out = understand; solve.

questions.question: She _____ her illness. (recovered from)
questions.explanation: Get over = recover from.

questions.question: He _____ to pressure. (yielded)
questions.explanation: Give in = yield; submit.

questions.question: I _____ in Prague. (became adult)
questions.explanation: Grow up = become adult.

questions.question: _____ a second. (wait)
questions.explanation: Hang on = wait.

questions.question: _____ the good work. (maintain)
questions.explanation: Keep up = maintain; continue.

questions.question: We need to _____ the problem. (resolve)
questions.explanation: Sort out = resolve; organise.

questions.question: She didn't _____. (arrive)
questions.explanation: Show up / turn up = arrive; appear.

questions.question: _____! (be careful)
questions.explanation: Look out / Watch out = be careful.

questions.question: Wrong: 'We put on the meeting.' (meaning postponed) Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Put off = postpone. Put on = wear.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Turn on it.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Separable: pronoun goes between verb and particle.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I will find the information.' (meaning search for in a book) Correct using a phrasal verb.
answers: I will look up the information.
I will look up the information
questions.explanation: Look up = search for information.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Who will look the children after?' Correct the sentence.
answers: Who will look after the children?
Who will look after the children
questions.explanation: Look after is inseparable: object after particle.

questions.question: Wrong: 'We ran out milk.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Run out of = use up. We need 'of' before the thing.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Don't give in!' (meaning don't stop trying) Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Give up = stop trying. Give in = yield.

questions.question: Wrong: 'She came up a solution.' Correct the sentence.
answers: She came up with a solution.
She came up with a solution
questions.explanation: Come up with = think of (an idea).

questions.question: Wrong: 'They cancelled off the match.' Correct using the correct phrasal verb.
questions.explanation: Call off = cancel. We say 'call off', not 'cancel off'.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Pick up it.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Separable: pronoun in the middle (Pick it up).

questions.question: Wrong: 'I found out my keys.' (meaning I was searching for them) Correct the sentence.
answers: I was looking for my keys.
questions.explanation: Find out = discover. Look for = search for something.
